intro: If you have a grid of values and want to spot patterns at a glance, a heatmap from data is the right chart. Heatmaps use color intensity to encode numbers, so high and low values jump out immediately without reading every cell. Excel and Google Sheets can both produce them, though the setup takes a few steps. If you want something faster, Distill builds a heatmap automatically when you upload a CSV.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Heatmap?
whatIs.body: A heatmap is a chart that represents individual values in a matrix using color. Darker or warmer colors typically indicate higher values, and lighter or cooler colors indicate lower ones. Heatmaps work best when you have two categorical dimensions (like day of week and hour of day) and one numeric measure to display at each intersection.

whenToUse.heading: When Should You Use a Heatmap?
bullets: You want to show activity patterns across two dimensions, such as website traffic by day and hour.
You need to compare performance across multiple categories at once, such as sales by region and product line.
You are looking for clusters or outliers in a large matrix of numbers that would be hard to read as a table.

excelSteps.heading: How to Create a Heatmap in Excel
steps: Step 1. Arrange your data in a matrix with row labels in column A, column headers in row 1, and numeric values filling the remaining cells.
Step 2. Select the range that contains only the numeric values (not the labels).
Step 3. Go to Home, click Conditional Formatting, and choose Color Scales.
Step 4. Pick a two-color or three-color scale. A common choice is red for high values, white for the midpoint, and blue for low values.
Step 5. Add your row and column labels back by selecting and formatting them as bold so the grid is readable.
Step 6. Optionally, shrink the row height and column width so the colored cells form a tight grid that looks like a traditional heatmap.

sheetsSteps.heading: How to Create a Heatmap in Google Sheets
steps: Step 1. Lay out your data in the same matrix format: row categories in column A, column categories in row 1, and numeric values in the body.
Step 2. Select the numeric values in the matrix body.
Step 3. Open the Format menu and click Conditional formatting.
Step 4. In the sidebar, switch the format rules dropdown to Color scale.
Step 5. Set the Minpoint, Midpoint (optional), and Maxpoint colors to create the gradient you want.
Step 6. Click Done. Resize the cells to make them square if you want the chart to look like a grid rather than a wide table.

distillSection.heading: A Faster Way: Skip the Manual Steps with Distill
distillSection.body: Conditional formatting in Excel or Sheets works, but it takes setup, and resizing cells to look right adds extra time. Distill skips all of that. Upload a CSV and Distill detects the structure of your data, auto-selects the right chart types, and generates a heatmap along with the other relevant visualizations. You get a shareable dashboard in seconds, with no formulas, no formatting rules, and no account required for CSV files up to 1,000 rows.

gifAlt: A screen recording showing a CSV file being dragged into Distill, which then automatically generates a heatmap and other charts from the uploaded data.

faqs.q: Can I create a heatmap without Excel?
faqs.a: Yes. Google Sheets supports color-scale conditional formatting that produces the same effect. Distill is another option: upload your CSV and it builds the heatmap for you with no spreadsheet setup needed.

faqs.q: What data format does a heatmap require?
faqs.a: A heatmap needs data in a matrix format: rows and columns each represent one dimension, and each cell holds a single numeric value. If your data is in a flat list with separate columns for each dimension, you will need to pivot it into a matrix first before applying color scales in Excel or Sheets.
Yes. Distill's free tier supports CSV files up to 1,000 rows and includes all chart types. No account is required to try it.
